question: The management control process involves four steps. In order, they are:
options: Plan → Hire → Train → Evaluate

Set performance standards → Measure actual performance → Compare performance to standards → Take corrective action if needed; this is the 'feedback control loop' fundamental to management; examples: a sales manager sets a monthly quota ($1M revenue) → measures actual sales weekly → compares actual to quota → if falling short, diagnoses root cause and adjusts (more calls, better pricing, sales training)
Budget → Spend → Audit → Report
Delegate → Monitor → Reward → Punish
explanation: Control process (Fayol, updated): the four-step cycle enables management to detect and correct deviation from plans before they become serious. The critical insight: control works backward — you need to set standards (goals, budgets, quality targets) before you can measure performance; you need measurement before comparison; comparison reveals deviation; deviation analysis reveals whether to adjust execution or revise the standard. The control loop that most organizations use: KPIs (key performance indicators) are the 'standards'; dashboards are the 'measurement'; exceptions reports are the 'comparison'; management review meetings are the 'corrective action' conversation.

question: 'Feedforward control' differs from 'feedback control' in that it:
options: Feedforward control provides feedback to employees about past performance

Feedforward control anticipates and prevents problems BEFORE they occur; feedback control detects and corrects problems AFTER they occur; examples: feedforward: an airplane pilot checks weather before departure (preventing flight into a storm rather than correcting mid-flight); a manufacturer inspects incoming raw materials before production (preventing defective materials from entering the process); feedback: a financial audit after the quarter ends; customer complaint resolution after the service failure; the value of feedforward: some problems are too costly or dangerous to correct after the fact (a nuclear plant's safety checks are feedforward — you don't discover safety problems by having accidents)
All quality control is feedforward control by definition
Feedforward control is only used in manufacturing environments
explanation: Control timing comparison: (1) Feedforward (preventive): checks inputs before the process begins; most effective for: high-consequence failures, processes where downstream correction is expensive or impossible. (2) Concurrent (real-time): monitors during the process; examples: surgical nurse counting instruments during an operation; statistical process control monitoring production in real time. (3) Feedback (corrective): evaluates outputs after completion; most common; examples: quality inspection at end of production, financial reporting after the period. The management principle: the higher the cost of failure, the more the control system should emphasize feedforward over feedback. Aviation: preflight checklists (feedforward) are extensive because in-flight corrections are dangerous; pharmaceutical manufacturing: extensive incoming material testing (feedforward) because defective product reaching patients is unacceptable.

question: A 'Balanced Scorecard' (Kaplan and Norton) controls organizational performance by measuring:
options: Only financial metrics since those are what shareholders care about

Four perspectives simultaneously: (1) Financial — are we creating shareholder value? (revenue, profit margin, ROI); (2) Customer — how do customers perceive us? (NPS, retention, market share); (3) Internal Processes — are our key processes efficient and effective? (cycle time, defect rate, on-time delivery); (4) Learning and Growth — are we developing capabilities for the future? (employee training, innovation pipeline, IT capability); the insight: financial measures are lagging indicators (they tell you what happened); customer and process measures are leading indicators (they predict future financial performance)
Annual employee performance reviews only
Competitor performance benchmarked against the company's own metrics
explanation: Balanced Scorecard's practical value: companies that only measure financial performance discover problems when it's too late to correct the root cause. Example: Sears discovered in the late 1990s that its auto service centers were performing poorly (customer perspective) before this showed up in falling revenue (financial perspective). If Sears had measured customer satisfaction leading indicators, they could have intervened before the financial decline. The BSC connection: financial outcomes are driven by customer satisfaction, which is driven by internal process quality, which is driven by employee capability and IT systems. By measuring all four, managers can intervene at the root cause level rather than reacting to financial outcomes after they've already declined.

question: 'Management by Exception' (MBE) is the control principle that:
options: Managers should personally review all performance data regardless of performance level

Managers should focus attention on significant deviations from plan (exceptions) rather than reviewing all performance data — when performance is within acceptable range, no action is needed; the principle improves management efficiency: a CEO of a 50-business conglomerate cannot review every business unit's performance in detail; exception reporting flags the businesses with significant variance (positive or negative) for management attention; Amazon's dashboards are designed around exceptions: everything within normal parameters is 'green'; only yellow or red flagged items require management response
Only poor performance should be reported to management, never positive performance
Management by exception applies only to financial controls
explanation: MBE in practice: exception reporting reduces the cognitive burden of controlling large organizations. Without MBE: a regional manager with 15 stores reviews all 15 stores' weekly performance data (overwhelming). With MBE: the system flags the 3 stores with >10% variance (positive or negative) from their targets; the manager focuses on understanding what drove the variance and taking action. The Amazon operational reviews (Weekly Business Review): each category is reviewed only if metrics fall outside control limits; routine performance is tracked but not subject to detailed management review. The risk: exception-only reporting means management may miss systemic trends that individually look 'within control' but collectively indicate a strategic problem.

question: A retailer uses 'clan control' — strong shared values and culture — rather than formal rules and procedures to ensure employees provide excellent customer service. Nordstrom's famously thin employee handbook ('Rule #1: Use good judgment in all situations. There will be no other rules.') exemplifies this approach. What does clan control require to work?
options: Clan control is impossible without extensive written policies and procedures

Strong socialization, careful hiring, and shared values: clan control relies on internalized norms rather than rules — employees know what good looks like and are motivated to achieve it without being told explicitly; requires: (1) Hiring for values fit (people who genuinely care about customer service); (2) Intensive socialization (new hires learn 'how we do things here' through modeling and mentorship, not manuals); (3) Peer accountability (team members hold each other to the standard rather than depending on management enforcement); (4) Trust that judgment will be exercised appropriately; the risk: clan control breaks down when: values are not clearly shared (new hires from different cultures); growth is too fast to socialize properly; a leader demonstrates values-inconsistent behavior
Clan control is more expensive to administer than formal rules-based control
All organizations should use clan control because it eliminates the need for management
explanation: Clan control vs. bureaucratic control: bureaucratic control works through rules and procedures (McDonald's operations manual: exactly how to build a Big Mac, how to handle a complaint, exactly what temperature to hold the fries). Clan control works through shared values and norms. Nordstrom's model: the 'good judgment' principle allows Nordstrom employees to: refund items purchased at competitors, accept clearly worn merchandise, and spend hours finding a customer the right product — behaviors that would be impossible to specify in a rule book but are obviously 'the Nordstrom way' to a well-socialized employee. The Nordstrom challenge: as the company grew and hired rapidly, socialization quality declined, and the 'Nordstrom service' reputation eroded. Clan control scales slowly — each socialization connection is personal.

question: Boeing's 737 MAX disaster (2018-2019) involved flawed MCAS (Maneuvering Characteristics Augmentation System) software that caused two crashes killing 346 people. The FAA allowed Boeing to self-certify certain aircraft systems (rather than independently testing), and Boeing's internal safety culture had been weakened by financial pressure and organizational changes following a merger. What control system failures does this represent?
options: The 737 MAX crashes were caused entirely by pilot error and had no organizational control dimension

Multiple control system failures: (1) Self-regulation failure — FDA-style regulatory capture: allowing Boeing to certify its own aircraft safety created a conflict of interest; 'the company certifying the safety of a product it's trying to sell as quickly as possible will face pressure to approve'; (2) Concurrent control failure — Boeing engineers raised concerns about MCAS behavior in simulators before certification; these concerns were not adequately escalated; organizational pressure to avoid expensive simulator training requirements (which would have required airlines to retrain pilots) suppressed internal safety objections; (3) Feedback loop failure — early warning signs in testing were not addressed; design decisions (making MCAS rely on a single sensor) violated aviation redundancy principles but weren't caught by oversight; (4) Incentive misalignment — stock buyback culture and performance pressure prioritized schedule over safety review rigor
Boeing's control systems were entirely adequate and the crashes resulted only from software bugs
Aviation safety regulations are always sufficient to prevent control system failures
explanation: Boeing 737 MAX control system analysis: the Congressional investigation identified: (1) FAA was effectively captured by Boeing (FAA relied on Boeing employees to certify Boeing's own planes); (2) Boeing's culture shifted from 'safety engineering culture' to 'schedule and cost culture' after the McDonnell Douglas merger (1997); (3) MCAS design decision: to avoid FAA-required simulator training (which would have cost airlines billions and potentially lost orders), Boeing decided to make MCAS unnoticeable to pilots — hiding its existence undermined the human override control mechanism; (4) Single-sensor design: MCAS relied on a single angle-of-attack sensor; redundancy (two sensors with disagreement detection) would have prevented the crashes. Each failure traces to a broken control mechanism: preventive (design review), concurrent (testing oversight), feedback (post-anomaly investigation), and cultural (authority to escalate safety concerns).

question: A subscription business tracks 'churn rate' (percentage of subscribers who cancel each month). The company sets a target of <2% monthly churn. In January, churn is 3.5%. The operations team digs into the data and finds: 60% of churners cite 'price too high' as the primary reason, 25% cite 'don't use it enough,' 15% cite 'technical problems.' What control system principle applies to the diagnosis and response?
options: The company should immediately lower prices since 60% cited price as the reason

Root cause analysis before corrective action: the 3.5% churn is the deviation; the control loop requires diagnosing WHY before prescribing the corrective action; the root cause distribution shows three distinct problems requiring three different responses: 'price too high' (60%) — options: targeted retention offers, value communication improvement, or product-price reframing; 'don't use enough' (25%) — options: engagement feature improvement, usage reminders, customer success outreach; 'technical problems' (15%) — options: bug fix prioritization, uptime investment; applying one solution (price reduction) to a 60/25/15 split ignores the 40% whose churn has nothing to do with price; the correct control response: separate intervention streams for each root cause, with measurement of each stream's effectiveness
Churn exceeding target should automatically trigger a price reduction
Control systems should only track one metric at a time to avoid confusion
explanation: Root cause analysis in control systems: the control loop identifies deviation (3.5% > 2% target); then the diagnostic phase (why is this happening?) informs the corrective action. Without root cause analysis, managers apply one-size-fits-all interventions to multi-causal problems. The 5-Why technique: why are subscribers churning → they say price is too high → why do they perceive the price as high → they're not using features that justify the price → why aren't they using those features → they weren't properly onboarded → the root cause is onboarding failure, not price. In this example, addressing the price concern without fixing the onboarding problem produces churners who still don't use the product and eventually decide the price is too high. Segmented root cause analysis produces segmented, appropriate interventions.

question: An e-commerce company uses the following control system: real-time sales dashboards (updated hourly), weekly cross-functional business reviews, monthly financial closes with variance analysis, and quarterly strategic reviews with the board. Each level has different time horizons and decision types. What organizational principle does this structure embody?
options: Organizations should use only one type of control system to avoid confusion

Hierarchical control systems match control frequency to decision type and organizational level: real-time dashboards: operational decisions (when should we increase server capacity? should we change the ad bid for this keyword?); decisions made by frontline analysts and automated systems; time window: minutes to hours. Weekly business review: operational management decisions (why did conversion rate drop this week? what's causing inventory discrepancies?); decisions by VP/Director level; time window: 1–4 weeks. Monthly financial close: financial management decisions (are we on track to hit quarterly plan? where is margin being compressed?); decisions by CFO and business unit heads; time window: 1–3 months. Quarterly board review: strategic decisions (is the business model working? should we change strategic direction?); decisions by CEO and board; time window: 6–18 months; the principle: the frequency of control must match the frequency of meaningful decisions — hourly board reviews would be noise; annual operational reviews would be far too slow
All organizational decisions should be made during annual strategic planning cycles
Real-time data always produces better organizational decisions than periodic reporting
explanation: Nested control loops: effective management control systems design separate loops for different decision timescales. Amazon's operational system: 'Weekly Business Review' is a formal cadence where every business team's key metrics are reviewed; significant variances are owned and explained. This creates accountability without requiring continuous meetings. The tactical vs. strategic distinction: operational teams need real-time feedback to make minute-by-minute decisions (inventory allocation, ad bidding, server scaling); strategic decisions require long enough time windows to distinguish signal from noise (monthly or quarterly). Feedback frequency mismatch: if you evaluate a marketing campaign after one day, you're making decisions based on statistical noise; after 30 days, you have meaningful signal. Control system design requires explicit decisions about what questions each control loop is designed to answer.

question: A call center manager has 3 monitoring options: (A) randomly listen to 5% of all calls; (B) review only calls that result in customer complaints; (C) continuously monitor all agents via screen recording and voice recording with real-time supervisor visibility. Option C increases agent stress and turnover, but reduces customer complaints. What control philosophy trade-off does this present?
options: Maximum surveillance always produces optimal outcomes in customer service environments

Output control vs. behavior control trade-off with human performance implications: Option A (random sampling = statistical output control): monitors outcomes with minimal intrusion; requires that agents have sufficient autonomy and competence to perform well without continuous observation; high trust, low surveillance. Option B (complaint-only = negative-feedback control): only measures failures; misses improving inputs; reactive not preventive; misses the 95% of bad calls that don't generate formal complaints. Option C (full surveillance = behavior control): monitors all inputs (every conversation); prevents problems before they become complaints; but: the Hawthorne effect is double-edged — surveillance increases compliance but reduces intrinsic motivation; when agents feel surveilled, they follow scripts rather than exercising judgment; higher turnover means higher training costs; the optimal design: behavior control for new agents (they need monitoring to develop); output control for experienced agents (give them autonomy once proven); targeted behavior control when output metrics show unexplained decline
Call center managers should always implement maximum monitoring regardless of trade-offs
Employee monitoring never affects performance quality
explanation: Behavior vs. output control (Ouchi's framework): behavior control (specifying HOW work is done) appropriate when: the relationship between behavior and output is well understood; outputs are difficult to measure; the work is complex enough that wrong behavior produces difficult-to-detect outcomes. Output control (specifying WHAT should be achieved) appropriate when: outputs are easily measured; the relationship between behavior and output is not well understood; autonomy in how to achieve results is important for quality or motivation. The call center example: for frontline employees, a hybrid is often best — monitor calls during training (behavior control), then shift to output metrics (customer satisfaction scores, first-call resolution) as competency is established. Full ongoing surveillance consistently produces the higher-turnover/script-following outcome documented in multiple call center studies.

question: Google's 'Objectives and Key Results' (OKR) system — used at Google since 1999, originating from Intel — sets goals as inspiring objectives ('make the Chrome browser the fastest browser in the world') with measurable key results ('reduce page load time by 20%', 'achieve 40% market share', 'pass Octane benchmark score by 50 points'). A defining feature: OKRs are set so ambitiously that achieving 70% is considered success; achieving 100% suggests the goal was too easy. How does this control system design counteract goal-setting pathologies?
options: Setting goals that can't be reached 100% is always a bad management practice

OKR design counters gaming and sandbagging: traditional goal-setting creates sandbagging (setting easily achievable goals to guarantee bonuses) and gaming (hitting the metric in ways that don't achieve the underlying objective); OKR's 70% success design: (1) Eliminates sandbagging — if 100% achievement means the goal was too easy, managers are disincentivized to set low bars; (2) Separates OKRs from compensation — Google deliberately does not link OKR achievement directly to compensation to avoid gaming; if 70% = success, there's no financial penalty for missing 30%, removing the incentive to game metrics; (3) Separates strategy (OKR aspiration) from execution (what work gets done this quarter to advance toward the OKR); (4) Creates a 'moonshot' culture — ambitious goals that fully achieved would be described as industry-defining achievements, not incremental improvements
Setting ambitious OKRs always guarantees achievement of 100% of them
OKRs are used only for individual employee performance management, not team or company goals
explanation: OKR system design (John Doerr's 'Measure What Matters'): the 70% achievement norm is counterintuitive but important. The two failure modes of ambitious goal-setting it addresses: (1) Sandbagging: if compensation is tied to goal achievement, managers set achievable goals and hit them easily → no stretch, no innovation; (2) Fear of ambition: if missing goals = career risk, managers won't set genuinely challenging goals → incrementalism. OKR's innovation: ambition is valued over achievement percentage. A team that sets 'become the best search engine in the world' and achieves 70% of that is better than a team that sets 'improve search recall by 2%' and achieves 100%. The compensation separation is critical: Google's OKR scores don't directly drive bonuses (separate performance process). This allows OKR to measure ambition and progress without creating gaming incentives.
